problem_summary: 2022 MCM Problem C (data): given five years of daily gold (LBMA) and Bitcoin price series, build a model that uses only data up to the current day to decide each day whether to hold cash, gold, or Bitcoin, maximize the final value of an initial $1000 (with 1% gold / 2% Bitcoin transaction costs), prove the strategy is best, analyze sensitivity to transaction cost, and write a trader memo. This team reframed the single-asset allocation problem as a relative-value/statistical-arbitrage problem and built a cointegration-driven pairs-trading strategy ("Modified Dollar Neutral", MDN), reporting $1000 -> ~$106,986 over 2016-09-10 to 2021-09-10.
models_used: Pairs trading / statistical arbitrage as the core strategy, treating gold and Bitcoin as the two legs of a long-short pair

Modified Dollar Neutral (MDN) strategy - their named extension of dollar-neutral pairs trading with a total-position-limit constraint (P_A|Q_A|+P_B|Q_B|=2I) and a currency-neutrality constraint (P_A Q_A + P_B Q_B = 0)

Cointegration analysis (Engle-Granger-style CI(d,b) theory) with the Johansen cointegration test (VAR-based, trace statistic LR_M = -n*sum log(1-lambda_i)) to establish a lagged equilibrium relationship between the two price series
ADF / unit-root stationarity testing plus ACF inspection on levels and first/second differences

Three explicit baseline models for comparison: (1) Monte Carlo random-walk simulation strategy S_{t+1}=S_t*exp(mu*dt + sigma*z), (2) XGBoost gradient-boosted decision trees on engineered features, (3) attention-based LSTM (SA-LSTM) on 30-day x 20-feature windows
Markowitz efficient-frontier / mean-variance framing for the risk-aversion extension, with a utility function U = alpha_n - lambda_A*phi_p^2 - TC and risk-aversion coefficient lambda_A = IR/(2*phi_p)

A reinvestment-risk 'non-trading interval' model derived from marginal contribution to value-added (MCVA) and marginal contribution to active risk (MCAR = d sigma_p / d w_n), giving the no-trade band IR*MCAR_n - SC_n + R_f <= R_n <= IR*MCAR_n + PC_n + R_f
Closed-form transaction-cost decay model a_n = a_0*(1+alpha)^{-n} and (1 - alpha*k/(1+alpha))^n * a_0 = a_n, fitted against the simulated cost sweep
key_techniques: Custom Python backtesting engine (TradeEnv class with data_dict, Brokerage, Account, History and Getdata/Trade/Record methods) that feeds the strategy only past-up-to-today prices, structurally enforcing the 'no lookahead' requirement of the problem
Strict anti-data-leakage train/test split: first 200 days used only for ML training, last ~1600 days used only for backtesting, drawn explicitly as a timeline figure
L2-norm normalization of both price series so the two legs are directly comparable for deviation/mean-reversion signals

Domain-aware missing-value handling: gold has non-trading days while Bitcoin trades daily, so gold gaps are forward-filled with the last valid price (justified by 'non-trading-day prices are unaffected by the market')

Feature engineering for the ML baselines (log return, past returns, momentum P_t - P_{t-k}, SMA, EMA, MACD, RSI, KDJ) followed by t-SNE separability check and a correlation heatmap for feature selection

A rich, multi-axis quantitative scorecard rather than a single metric: arithmetic return, geometric time-weighted return, return volatility, Jensen's alpha (with the clever assumption r_M = r_f since gold/BTC are uncorrelated with equity indices, making alpha == excess return), Sharpe ratio, information ratio, and yearly tracking error
Horizontal comparison (strategy vs 3 baselines) plus vertical/internal robustness comparison (sweeping the cointegration lag period and finding a stable plateau on [15,60] days, peak around 40 days)

Sensitivity analysis that pairs an analytical derivation (geometric decay of value with number of trades and cost rate) with a simulated curve, then shows the fitted curve matches the true values - using theory to validate the simulation rather than just plotting numbers
why_it_won: From a judge's perspective this paper wins on problem reframing and disciplined comparison. (1) Reframing: instead of the obvious daily portfolio-weight allocation that most teams attempted, they recognized a lagged cointegration relationship between gold and Bitcoin and turned a single-series forecasting problem into a relative-value pairs-trade - a genuinely insightful, defensible modeling choice that also sidesteps the hard problem of predicting absolute prices. (2) Rigor of the 'prove it's best' requirement: they did not just assert superiority; they built three credible competitors (random/Monte Carlo, XGBoost, attention-LSTM) inside the same backtesting harness and beat them on a panel of 6-7 finance-standard metrics (Sharpe 1.46 vs <1, information ratio ~3x, higher Jensen alpha and geometric return), giving quantitative, apples-to-apples evidence. (3) Methodological credibility: explicit anti-data-leakage split, a real backtesting engine that enforces causality, ADF + Johansen tests to justify the cointegration premise, and a lag-period robustness sweep. (4) The memo argues the strategy is a transparent 'white box' that is robust to noisy/limited data, which is exactly the right narrative for the messy Problem C dataset and shows judge-pleasing self-awareness about why a simpler math model can beat fashionable ML. (5) Strong narrative coherence and finance literacy throughout signal a team that understands both the math and the domain.
section_highlights: The Summary front-loads the headline result ($1000 -> $106,986, ~107x) and the comparative ratios (Sharpe 1.53-2.32x, excess return 1.49-3.17x other strategies), giving judges the punchline immediately. Section 4 builds the modeling case logically: data observation -> spotting that BTC lags gold -> cointegration theory -> Johansen test -> the MDN algorithm box (Algorithm 1 with clear open/close-position logic). Section 5 is the strongest section: it constructs the three baselines, shows the data-leakage-free split and the attention-LSTM architecture as figures, then delivers two clean comparison tables (basic indicators Table 1, developing indicators Table 2) plus net-worth and tracking-error charts - a textbook 'establish baselines, then dominate them on many metrics' structure. Section 6 adds depth most teams skip: decomposing market risk into price risk vs reinvestment risk and deriving a no-trade interval, plus an investor-risk-aversion utility extension. Section 7's sensitivity analysis is notable for deriving the cost-decay formula analytically and then showing the fitted curve overlays the simulated true values. The trader Memo is well-targeted: it explicitly frames the choice of a transparent math model over an ML 'black box' as a virtue for noisy data, and explains the backtesting system in plain language.
pitfalls_or_limitations: The figure data in the markdown is clearly OCR/transcription noise (e.g. Figure 15/19 'returns' shown as flat or as percentages up to 120%, ACF tables, heatmap values) so the exact plotted numbers are unreliable; the result narrative is consistent but the reproduced tables should not be trusted at face value

Overfitting / survivorship-of-hindsight risk: a 107x return over a period where Bitcoin itself rose enormously raises the question of how much of the edge is genuine mean-reversion alpha vs simply being long a hugely appreciating asset - the paper even notes the strategy 'exceeded Bitcoin's price growth' but does not rigorously rule out that the cointegration signal is just a momentum/long-BTC proxy

The economic premise is thin: gold and Bitcoin having a 'lagged cointegration relationship' is asserted from one Johansen test, and the result that BTC lags gold is treated as stable and tradable, which is a strong and debatable financial claim; cointegration over 2016-2021 may be spurious or regime-dependent

Assumptions are quite permissive (no transaction delay, infinitely divisible trades, no slippage, no short-selling/leverage), and while transaction cost sensitivity is analyzed, market-impact and liquidity are ignored

The ML baselines may be deliberately under-tuned strawmen: a single 200-day training window for XGBoost/LSTM on a 5-year backtest is very little data, which both supports their 'ML is data-hungry' memo argument and conveniently weakens the competitors
Jensen's alpha simplification r_M = r_f is convenient but conflates 'excess return over risk-free' with 'alpha', overstating skill attribution

The MDN constraints (dollar-neutral, total position = 2I) sit awkwardly with Assumption 3 that short-selling is disallowed - a true dollar-neutral long-short book requires shorting, so there is an internal inconsistency between the stated assumptions and the named strategy
reusable_patterns: Reframe the given problem into a better-posed one: turning 'allocate a portfolio daily' into 'trade the relative-value spread between two cointegrated assets' is a transferable move - look for structure (cointegration, correlation, conservation) that converts hard absolute prediction into easier relative prediction

Build a single backtesting/simulation harness first, then run the proposed model AND all baselines through the identical harness - this makes the 'prove it's best' comparison airtight and is reusable for any Problem-C-style data competition

Always include explicit, named baselines spanning a spectrum (a naive/random one, a classic-ML one like XGBoost, a deep-learning one like LSTM) so 'our model is best' becomes a measured claim, not an assertion

Use a panel of domain-standard evaluation metrics (here: Sharpe, information ratio, Jensen alpha, geometric return, tracking error, volatility) rather than one number, and present them in side-by-side tables - judges reward multi-axis evidence
Enforce no-lookahead/no-data-leakage explicitly and show it as a figure (train window vs backtest window timeline); making causality visible is a credibility multiplier in any time-series task

Pair an analytical derivation with a simulation in sensitivity analysis and show they agree (fitted curve over true values) - using closed-form math to validate empirical results is more convincing than plotting alone

Do a robustness sweep over the model's own key hyperparameter (here the cointegration lag) and report the stable operating range [15,60] days - demonstrating you know where the model holds and where it breaks
Domain-justified data cleaning: explain WHY a fill/imputation choice is valid in the problem's context (gold non-trading days), not just that you did it

Frame the modeling choice as a narrative in the memo: 'transparent white-box math model beats fragile ML black-box on noisy/limited data' is a persuasive, decision-maker-friendly story that also doubles as a limitation-aware justification
